EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking northeast along the Atlantic coast crossed the Gulf of Maine during the 14th...before exiting through the maritimes during the 15th. Heavy snow developed during the morning of the 14th...continued overnight...then ended during the morning of the 15th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were reported at times across central and Downeast Maine. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches across Downeast Maine...with 7 to 12 inches across central areas. Winds of 15 to 25 mph contributed to significant blowing and drifting snow...with near whiteout conditions at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure weakened as it crossed the Great Lakes on the morning of the 21st as a second area of low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast.  The coastal low intensified rapidly as it moved northeast along the East Coast to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 22nd. The coastal low brought heavy snow to the entire state with amounts generally ranging from 10 to 15 inches in coastal areas to 20 to 30 inches in the mountains.  Eustis reported a total of 41 inches of snow from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy late April rains...the influx of water from tributary rivers and the melting of snow in headwater areas led to flooding of the Penobscot River at Eddington. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas bordering the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of 3.00 to 4.00 inches of rain which fell in late April...the influx of water from tributary rivers and the melting of snow in headwater areas led to flooding of the Penobscot River at Eddington. The river crested at around 1.50 feet above flood stage. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas bordering the river. The monetary value represents the total damage to public infrastructure across all of Penobscot county from the late April and early May flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms which developed over New Hampshire in the late morning and early afternoon pushed into western Maine by mid afternoon. These storms had lost some their earlier intensity but still produced numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm snapped multiple trees in Waterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Dingley building in Lewiston and caused a moderate amount of damage to the building's wooden tower.  The building housed the headquarters of the Lewiston School Department.  The lightning also caused damage to the building's phone, electrical, and elevator systems.
Lightning struck a home in Sanford igniting a fire that caused heavy damage to the attic and roof.  The first and second floors sustained water damage.  Damage was estimated at $60,000.
Lightning struck a home in Sanford igniting a fire that caused heavy damage to the second floor, attic and roof.  Damage was estimated at $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Ohio River Valley and into New England, spreading heavy rain over the region. Rainfall began on the afternoon of the 28th of April and ended during the evening of the 29th. Rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 6 inches causing minor flooding on the Ellis, Swift, Androscoggin, and Kennebec Rivers. Flooding on the Androscoggin and Kennebec rivers lasted into May 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at the end of April resulted in flooding on the Androscoggin River at Auburn, (flood stage 13.0 ft.), which crested at 13.69 ft. The river fell below flood stage shortly after midnight on May 1st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at the end of April caused flooding on the Kennebec River at North Sidney, (flood stage 17.0 ft.), which crested at 22.08 ft. Flooding lasted into mid-morning on May 1st when the river fell below flood stage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at the end of April caused flooding on the Kennebec River at Augusta, (flood stage 12.0 ft.), which crested at 18.42 ft. Flooding lasted into the evening of May 1st when the river fell below flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Ohio Valley and into New England, spreading heavy rain over the region. Rainfall began on the afternoon of April 28th and ended during the evening of April 29th. Rainfall and rainfall rates were high enough to cause serious county wide flooding of small rivers and streams in the midcoast counties of Waldo, Somerset, Knox, and Lincoln. Rainfall amounts in the damaged areas ranged from 3 to 6 inches. Damage to roads in the area was extensive with blown out culverts and washed out roadbeds. Damage also occurred to some water control facilities and bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused serious county-wide flooding of small rivers and streams throughout Lincoln County. Some of the most extensive damage occurred in the towns of Bremen, Bristol, Damariscotta, Jefferson, Newcastle, and Waldoboro. The most damage was reported in Waldoboro where there was $55,300.00 in flood damage.

Some of the hardest hit towns were Belmont, Brooks, Liberty, Licolnville, Monroe, Montville, Morrill, Searsport, Stockton Springs, and Thorndike. The most extensive damage occurred in Liberty where repair and clean-up costs were $144,400.00.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust estimated at 50 mph also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail developed across northern Maine in advance of a cold front crossing the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A localized microburst containing estimated winds of up to 85 mph damaged a large metal silo at the intersection of Routes 163 and 167. An air conditioning unit at the same location was lifted from its slab and moved several inches from its original location. Several trees were also toppled adjacent to the property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail developed across northern Maine in advance of a cold front crossing the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled along Blake Street and at the University of Maine at Presque Isle campus by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed across northern Penobscot county producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled onto power lines near North Twin Lake by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

Thunderstorms with rainfall rates of 1.50 to 2.00 inches per hour produced localized flash flooding across southern Piscataquis county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorm rains caused the Packard Brook to overflow its banks...with the water covering portions of Route 150 south of Parkman.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorm rains washed out a portion of road at the intersection of Prospect and Elm streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in the town of Lyman during the late afternoon of of July 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail and some flash flooding in Franklin County. Another thunderstorm produced a mudslide and road washout in southern Oxford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced road washouts in Franklin County.  Center Hill Road in Weld was severely damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of heavy rain...along with embedded slow moving thunderstorms producing rainfall rates of 1.00 to 2.00 inches per hour...contributed to flash flooding across northwest Maine. Several small streams overflowed their banks leading to road closures and in some cases damage to roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge was washed out at Twin Brook on the Michaud Road due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable airmass remained over Maine on the 19th of July with a stalled frontal boundary draped across the northern portion of the state. Thunderstorms developed on an instability axis across southern sections of the state around mid afternoon. Large hail was mainly associated with these storms but larger cells also produced reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A servere thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail in Kennebunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure produced 2 to 3 inches of rainfall in northwestern Maine. A couple of brief periods of minor flooding resulted on the Swift River at Roxbury during the early morning and again in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of rapid tidal fluctuations hit the mid coast of Maine during the afternoon of October 28. In Boothbay Harbor, several fluctuations were observed with water levels rising and falling 3 to 4 feet in a matter of minutes.  The rapid changes in water level created strong currents that damaged docks and boats along the coast.

A large area of low pressure moved from the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 15th through upstate New York to north of Maine by the morning of the 16th.  At the time, the astronomical tidal variations were near a maximum. While little in the way of wind or waves occurred with this event, the combination of the astronomical tide and the surge associated with the storm caused minor flooding in Portland.  Tides reached 12.25 feet on the 15th and 12.28 feet on the 16th.  Flood level in Portland is 12 feet.  Damage to property was minimal and estimated at around $10,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An astronomically high tide and a storm surge resulted in minor flooding in the Portland area during the early afternoon of November 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds developed across Downeast Maine during the 25th in advance of intense low pressure crossing the region. Wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph were common along the Downeast coast...with a peak gust of 74 mph reported at Sedgwick Ridge and a gust of 67 mph at Brooklin both in Hancock county. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common across interior Downeast areas...with gusts to around 60 mph. The high winds toppled numerous trees and power lines across the region...contributing to extensive power outages and blocking roads. In Bangor...a tree fell on a house damaging the second story and a car. Around 15000 customers lost power across the region...with around 13000 of these customers in Hancock county. In Hancock county Mount Desert Island...Deer Isle and the towns of Blue Hill...Trenton and Stonington experiened the most significant power outages. These were many of the same areas impacted by significant power outages due to high winds around 10 days earlier. The power outages were so extensive across Maine that the Governor declared a State of Emergency. Utility workers from out of state were called in to help restore power. Some areas were without power for several days.|In Bar Harbor...several boats were torn from their moorings and driven aground. Several boats in other harbors along the Downeast coast were sunk at their moorings. Some piers and floats were also damaged by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds...with a peak gust of 62 mph measured at Cutler...toppled numerous trees and power lines across the region contributing to numerous power outages. A wind gust of 61 mph was measured at Jonesport...with numerous wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph measured across the region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding covered the Reality Road at the Umsaskis Thoroughfare crossing of the Allagash River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved through the Great Lakes and into the St. Lawrence Valley on Tuesday, November 25th.  Snow fell north of the associated warm front as the low moved slowly northeast.  Warmer air eventually worked its way northward and changed the precipitation to rain.  Heavy snow fell across Northern Oxford and Northern Franklin Counties with 13 inches reported in Phillips and 9 inches in Bethel.  Coastal and interior sections of western Maine had little or no snow as the precipitation fell mostly as rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking through the Gulf of Maine brought blizzard conditions to all of northern and eastern Maine. Blizzard conditions expanded northward across the region through the afternoon and evening of the 21st...then continued into the morning of the 22nd. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common from northern Maine to interior Downeast areas...with 40 to 60 mph wind gusts along the Downeast coast. The combination of wind and snow frequently reduced visibilities below a quarter mile...with near zero visibilities at times in whiteout conditions. Numerous schools and events across the region were cancelled due to the blizzard. The strong winds also forced several boats aground in the Lubec area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph combined with heavy snow to produce blizzard conditions. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 18 to 24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across Downeast Maine brought significant precipitation to all of northern and eastern Maine. Precipitation began during the afternoon of the 11th...then continued through the morning of the 12th. Enough warm air aloft was drawn across the region to allow the snow to transition to sleet and freezing rain. Across northern areas...storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 3 to 5 inches with .25 to .50 of an inch of ice accumulation. Across central and Downeast areas...storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches with .50 to 1.0 inch of ice accumulation. Numerous trees and power lines were toppled across the region due to the weight of the accumulated ice. Around 15000 customers lost power at various times during the event particularly across central and Downeast areas.|Strong onshore winds developed during the afternoon of the 12th along the Downeast coast...just south of the track of the low. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported along the Downeast coast...with a peak gust of 63 mph at Sedgwick Ridge. The strong winds combined with the ice to contribute to downed trees and power lines. The strong onshore winds...along with an astronomically high tide...combined to produce minor coastal flooding near Winter Harbor at the time of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches. Storm total ice accumulations were around 1/2 of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore winds developed in advance of a cold front crossing the region during the evening of the 15th. The strong winds toppled trees and power lines along portons of the Downeast coast. Winds gusted to 50 to 60 mph at times...with a peak gust of 61 mph measured at Cutler.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred in advance of a cold front crossing the region. The strong winds toppled trees and power lines across portions of the Downeast coast. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was measured at Cutler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in the wake of a cold front crossing the region during the morning of the 25th...then continued through the afternoon. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across the region. Along the Downeast coast...strong onshore winds also occurred in advance of the front with gusts to around 65 mph. The winds snapped branches... toppled trees and brought down power lines across all of northern and eastern Maine. Several thousand customers lost power at various times during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts up to 60 mph toppled trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking through the Gulf of Maine brought blizzard conditions to all of northern and eastern Maine. Blizzard conditions expanded northward across the region through the afternoon and evening of the 21st...then continued into the morning of the 22nd. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common from northern Maine to interior Downeast areas...with 40 to 60 mph wind gusts along the Downeast coast. The combination of wind and snow frequently reduced visibilities below a quarter mile...with near zero visibilities at times in whiteout conditions. Numerous schools and events across the region were cancelled due to the blizzard. The strong winds also forced several boats aground in the Lubec area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph combined with heavy snow to produce blizzard conditions. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved from near Chicago on the morning of November 24th, to near Buffalo on the morning of November 25th, spreading snow into Maine. Snow changed to rain during the afternoon of November 25th as the storm continued slowly moving northeast. Rainfall of 4 inches in the foothills of Maine in combination with melting snow caused small rivers and streams as well as some main stem rivers to rise rapidly. A portion of Elm Street in Mercer washed away when Indian Stream rose rapidly out of its banks. A driver in a jeep was washed into Indian Stream but was rescued and escaped injury. Intervale Road in Farmington was closed due to rising waters from the Sandy River as was Route 41 and George Thomas Road in New Sharon. Minor flooding was also reported on the Ellis River in South Andover.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Presumpscot River in Westbrook, (flood stage 15.0 ft), which crested at 17.78 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving through the Great Lakes produced storm force southerly winds and allowed for large waves to form which led to damage along the southwest coast of Maine. Damage in York County was estimated at $24,000 at three locations including Saco, Wells and Kennebunk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wells, Kennebunk and Saco beaches required snow plows to remove debris during the 918 am and 948 pm high tides on November 25th. High surf swept 500 yards of sand out to sea from a beach erosion project at Ferry Beach in Saco (Surf Street). Damage in York County came to $24,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved from near Chicago on the morning of November 24th, to near Buffalo on the morning of November 25th, spreading snow into Maine. Snow changed to rain during the afternoon of November 25th as the storm continued slowly moving northeast. Rainfall of 4 inches in the foothills of Maine in combination with melting snow caused small rivers and streams as well as some main stem rivers to rise rapidly. A portion of Elm Street in Mercer washed away when Indian Stream rose rapidly out of its banks. A driver in a jeep was washed into Indian Stream but was rescued and escaped injury. Intervale Road in Farmington was closed due to rising waters from the Sandy River as was Route 41 and George Thomas Road in New Sharon. Minor flooding was also reported on the Ellis River in South Andover.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Swift River at Roxbury, (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested 7.2 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning was listed as the probable cause of a fire that destroyed a home in Kingfield.  The fire apparently smouldered for about 12 hours before igniting.  Damage was estimated $200,000.

Low pressure tracking across Downeast Maine brought significant precipitation to all of northern and eastern Maine. Precipitation began during the afternoon of the 11th...then continued through the morning of the 12th. Enough warm air aloft was drawn across the region to allow the snow to transition to sleet and freezing rain. Across northern areas...storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 3 to 5 inches with .25 to .50 of an inch of ice accumulation. Across central and Downeast areas...storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches with .50 to 1.0 inch of ice accumulation. Numerous trees and power lines were toppled across the region due to the weight of the accumulated ice. Around 15000 customers lost power at various times during the event particularly across central and Downeast areas.|Strong onshore winds developed during the afternoon of the 12th along the Downeast coast...just south of the track of the low. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported along the Downeast coast...with a peak gust of 63 mph at Sedgwick Ridge. The strong winds combined with the ice to contribute to downed trees and power lines. The strong onshore winds...along with an astronomically high tide...combined to produce minor coastal flooding near Winter Harbor at the time of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 3 to 5 inches. Storm total ice accumulations were around 1/2 of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking through the Gulf of Maine brought blizzard conditions to all of northern and eastern Maine. Blizzard conditions expanded northward across the region through the afternoon and evening of the 21st...then continued into the morning of the 22nd. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were common from northern Maine to interior Downeast areas...with 40 to 60 mph wind gusts along the Downeast coast. The combination of wind and snow frequently reduced visibilities below a quarter mile...with near zero visibilities at times in whiteout conditions. Numerous schools and events across the region were cancelled due to the blizzard. The strong winds also forced several boats aground in the Lubec area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph combined with heavy snow to produce blizzard conditions. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in the wake of a cold front crossing the region during the morning of the 25th...then continued through the afternoon. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across the region. Along the Downeast coast...strong onshore winds also occurred in advance of the front with gusts to around 65 mph. The winds snapped branches... toppled trees and brought down power lines across all of northern and eastern Maine. Several thousand customers lost power at various times during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts up to 60 mph toppled trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed across northeast Aroostook county during the evening of the 9th in the wake of a cold front crossing the region. The strong gusty winds then persisted through the early morning hours of the 10th. Wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph were reported across northeast Aroostook county. The winds snapped trees and branches...with some trees uprooted. Trees and tree limbs falling on power lines contributed to power outages...with approximately 5,700 customers losing power for varying amounts of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph snapped tree limbs and trees...with some trees also uprooted. Trees and tree limbs falling on power lines contributed to power outages. Approximately 5,700 customers lost power for varying amounts of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Great Lakes weakened on New Year's Day as a secondary low formed off the North Carolina coast and then moved rapidly northeastward during the day as it intensified.  The low moved through the Gulf of Maine overnight to near Nova Scotia by 7 am, January 2nd. ||The storm brought heavy snow to much of the region, with a mix of snow and rain along the immediate coast of York and Cumberland Counties.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches across most of the area, with the highest amounts in the foothills where some areas received over a foot of snow.

Low pressure tracking northeast across Maine from the night of the 1st through the morning of the 2nd brought heavy snow and mixed precipitation to northern Maine. Precipitation began during the afternoon of the 1st...continued overnight...then ended during the late morning hours of the 2nd. The storm brought 7 to 12 inches of snow...along with a little sleet and freezing rain to Northern Aroostook county. Snow accumulations decreased further south across the region...with more of the precipitation falling in the form of sleet and freezing rain. Snow accumulations from southeast Aroostook to northern Penobscot counties ranged from 4 to 7 inches...with 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch of ice accumulation. Snow accumulations from central and southern portions of Piscataquis county...across central Penobscot county...to northern Washington county generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches...with 1/2 to 3/4 of an inch of ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

An area of low pressure moved slowly through the Great Lakes on the 13th as a secondary low formed off the North Carolina coast and then intensified as it moved slowly northeastward during the day and overnight.  The intensifying low moved slowly through the Gulf of Maine during the day on the 14th. ||The storm brought heavy snow to much of western Maine.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 7 inches over the mountains, but increased significantly over the foothills and interior zones where amounts ranged upward to 14 to 20 inches.  Coastal areas generally received between 9 and 12 inches of snow from the storm.

The low moved through the Gulf of Maine overnight to near Nova Scotia by 7 am, January 2nd. ||The storm brought heavy snow to much of the region, with a mix of snow and rain along the immediate coast of York and Cumberland Counties.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches across most of the area, with the highest amounts in the foothills where some areas received over a foot of snow.

An area of low pressure moved slowly through the Great Lakes on the 13th as a secondary low formed off the North Carolina coast and then intensified as it moved slowly northeastward during the day and overnight.  The intensifying low moved slowly through the Gulf of Maine during the day on the 14th. ||The storm brought heavy snow to much of western Maine.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 7 inches over the mountains, but increased significantly over the foothills and interior zones where amounts ranged upward to 14 to 20 inches.  Coastal areas generally received between 9 and 12 inches of snow from the storm.

Strong low pressure moved from near Chicago on the morning of November 24th, to near Buffalo on the morning of November 25th, spreading snow into Maine. Snow changed to rain during the afternoon of November 25th as the storm continued slowly moving northeast. Rainfall of 4 inches in the foothills of Maine in combination with melting snow caused small rivers and streams as well as some main stem rivers to rise rapidly. A portion of Elm Street in Mercer washed away when Indian Stream rose rapidly out of its banks. A driver in a jeep was washed into Indian Stream but was rescued and escaped injury. Intervale Road in Farmington was closed due to rising waters from the Sandy River as was Route 41 and George Thomas Road in New Sharon. Minor flooding was also reported on the Ellis River in South Andover.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the Sandy River to flood George Thomas Road and Route 41 in New Sharon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking northeast across Maine from the night of the 1st through the morning of the 2nd brought heavy snow and mixed precipitation to northern Maine. Precipitation began during the afternoon of the 1st...continued overnight...then ended during the late morning hours of the 2nd. The storm brought 7 to 12 inches of snow...along with a little sleet and freezing rain to Northern Aroostook county. Snow accumulations decreased further south across the region...with more of the precipitation falling in the form of sleet and freezing rain. Snow accumulations from southeast Aroostook to northern Penobscot counties ranged from 4 to 7 inches...with 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch of ice accumulation. Snow accumulations from central and southern portions of Piscataquis county...across central Penobscot county...to northern Washington county generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches...with 1/2 to 3/4 of an inch of ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 7 inches. Ice accumulations of up to 1/4 of an inch were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across the region during the morning of the 13th in advance of approaching low pressure. The snow transitioned to a wintry mix across central portions of the region during the afternoon of the 13th. Across Downeast areas...the snow transitioned to a wintry mix during the morning of the 13th before eventually transitioning to heavy rain from the late morning through early afternoon hours. The low tracked near the Maine coast during the evening of the 13th...with precipitation ending later that night while the low exited through the maritimes.|Across northern portions of Aroostook and Piscataquis Counties precipitation remained mostly snow...with some sleet and freezing rain. Storm total snow acumulations in this area generally ranged from 7 to 9 inches. From southeast Aroostook county...across northern portions of Penobscot and Washington counties...to central and southern Piscataquis counties storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches with locally higher totals. Ice accumulations across this region generally ranged from 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch...with up to 3/4 of an inch of ice reported across northern Washington county. Ice accumulations Downeast were generally less than 1/4 of an inch.|The combination of heavy rain and storm drains which had been blocked by snow and sleet contributed to standing water developing on some roads along with other poor drainage areas. The urban flooding was generally minor...though some roads were closed for a time.|The combination of gusty winds...along with heavy wet snow and ice accumulating on power lines and tree limbs which snapped...contributed to power outages. Approximately 4,500 customers across central and Downeast areas lost power for varying amounts of time during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches. Ice accumulations ranged from 1/10 to 1/4 of an inch...along with significant amounts of sleet.

Winds occasionally gusting to 30 to 40 mph developed toward the end of the event and continued after the snow had stopped...contributing to considerable blowing and drifting snow through the afternoon of the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several areas of low pressure moved along a quasi-stationary frontal boundary and produced a mixed precipitation event which changed to snow across the region as colder air filtered into the area.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches in southern and northern areas to about 4 to 10 inches in a band from Oxford County through Cumberland, Androscoggin, Kennebec and northern Waldo Counties.

Intensifying low pressure tracked from the southern New England coast to the coast of Maine during the 1st...before exiting through the maritimes during the 2nd. The storm brought heavy snow to much of northern and eastern Maine...with lighter snow across extreme northwest Maine. Snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour occurred at times with heavier snow bands which formed across portions of eastcentral Maine. Storm total snow accumulations of 10 to 15 inches were common across much of central and Downeast Maine. Totals of 12 to 18 inches...with local totals in excess of 20 inches...occurred across portions of eastcentral Maine where the heavier snow bands developed. Storm total snow accumulations across northeast Maine generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches. Winds of 15 to 25 mph contributed to significant blowing and drifting snow...with some gusts in excess of 30 mph near the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to Maine from the morning of December 11th to the morning of December 12th.  The greatest impact in the state was in coastal counties of the state where a significant ice storm occurred.  Following the ice storm, recovery and restoration efforts were negatively impacted by additional winter weather events that passed through the state.  ||The ice storm was caused by an area of low pressure that moved northeastward along a stalled frontal boundary off the East Coast.  Near ground level, cold air from eastern Canada funneled southward into the region on northerly winds.  Southerly winds at higher levels of the atmosphere brought warmer (above freezing) air in aloft.  Across southern sections of Maine, the thermal structure of the atmosphere was conducive to produce freezing rain and sleet event.   A mixture of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain began Thursday morning across the state. However, as the northerly winds strengthened at the surface and southerly winds strengthened aloft, the precipitation changed to mostly a mix of sleet and freezing rain across western Maine.  The freezing rain and sleet continued overnight and into Friday morning before ending.  While ���melted��� precipitation amounts across much of southwestern and coastal Maine ranged from 1 to 3 inches, ice accretion to trees and wires in the coastal counties generally ranged from about a half inch to about an inch.  The weight of the ice caused branches to snap, and trees to either snap or uproot, and brought down power lines and poles across the region.  Hundred of thousands of utility customers lost power during the event, with some customers without power for more than a week.  Central Maine Power Company reported about 220,000 customers without power during height of the storm.  Total damages across western and mid coast Maine were estimated to be more than $13 million with most of the damage in coastal areas.

However, as the northerly winds strengthened at the surface and southerly winds strengthened aloft, the precipitation changed to mostly a mix of sleet and freezing rain across western Maine.  The freezing rain and sleet continued overnight and into Friday morning before ending.  While ���melted��� precipitation amounts across much of southwestern and coastal Maine ranged from 1 to 3 inches, ice accretion to trees and wires in the coastal counties generally ranged from about a half inch to about an inch.  The weight of the ice caused branches to snap, and trees to either snap or uproot, and brought down power lines and poles across the region.  Hundred of thousands of utility customers lost power during the event, with some customers without power for more than a week.  Central Maine Power Company reported about 220,000 customers without power during height of the storm.  Total damages across western and mid coast Maine were estimated to be more than $13 million with most of the damage in coastal areas.

Intensifying low pressure tracked from the southern New England coast to the coast of Maine during the 1st...before exiting through the maritimes during the 2nd. The storm brought heavy snow to much of northern and eastern Maine...with lighter snow across extreme northwest Maine. Snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour occurred at times with heavier snow bands which formed across portions of eastcentral Maine. Storm total snow accumulations of 10 to 15 inches were common across much of central and Downeast Maine. Totals of 12 to 18 inches...with local totals in excess of 20 inches...occurred across portions of eastcentral Maine where the heavier snow bands developed. Storm total snow accumulations across northeast Maine generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches. Winds of 15 to 25 mph contributed to significant blowing and drifting snow...with some gusts in excess of 30 mph near the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure approaching from the Great Lakes re-developed along the Maine coast during the 1st. The low then rapidly intensified while tracking along the Maine coast to the maritimes from the night of the 1st through the 2nd. Snow developed during the morning of the 1st...with heavy snow expanding northward through the late morning and afternoon hours. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common...with localized rates occasionally reaching 3 to 4 inches per hour. The snow diminished...from south to north across the region...from late on the 1st through the 2nd. Storm total snow accumulations across the region generally ranged from 8 to 13 inches...though local totals to around 15 inches occurred across portions of Washington county beneath the more persistent heavier snow bands.|Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph...with occasional gusts in excess of 40 mph...contributed to extensive blowing and drifting snow during the 2nd with near whiteout conditions at times. Winds then diminished during the evening of the 2nd. The combination of gusty winds and snow weighing down tree limbs and power lines contributed to around 700 customers losing power in parts of Downeast Maine from late on the 1st into the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense slow moving area of low pressure located across northern New Brunswick and the Gulf of Saint Lawrence produced blizzard conditions across northern portions of Aroostook and Somerset counties through much of the 21st. Snow of varying intensity fell through the early afternoon of the 21st along with the strong winds. Strong winds then persisted through the rest of that afternoon and evening with a continuation of blizzard conditions with extensive blowing and drifting snow producing whiteout conditions across the region. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with frequent gusts of 40 to 50 mph...combined with storm total snow accumulations of 10 to 18 inches to produce the blizzard conditions. Blizzard conditions ended during the night of the 21st with diminishing winds.|Numerous roads were closed or had traffic limited during the 21st due to the hazardous driving conditions caused by extensive whiteouts and drifting snow. Several accidents were also attributed to the hazardous driving conditions which resulted in some injuries. The Aroostook county sheriff issued an emergency travel only request during the afternoon of the 21st due to the blizzard conditions.|The strong winds and heavy snow contributed to a garage collapse at Garfield Plantation and an industrial shed collapse at Fort Kent. A freight train became stuck in deep snow drifts near Grand Isle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with frequent gusts of 40 to 50 mph...combined with storm total snow accumulations of 10 to 18 inches to produce blizzard conditions. Numerous roads were closed or had traffic limited due to the hazardous conditions caused by extensive whiteouts and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense slow moving area of low pressure located across northern New Brunswick and the Gulf of Saint Lawrence produced high winds across portions of eastcentral and Downeast Maine. High winds were reported across southern portions of Piscataquis county along with central and southern portions of Penobscot county. Frequent wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph...with some estimated gusts to 60 mph...occurred through the morning into the evening of the 21st. |The winds tore the roof from the Maine Public Broadcasting Administrative Building in Bangor. Several thousand customers lost power for varying amounts of time across Downeast areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph...with some estimated gusts to 60 mph...toppled trees and power lines across the region. Some roads were blocked by fallen trees and utility poles. The fallen power lines and utility poles contributed to power outages.

Further south a greater portion of the precipitation fell in the form of sleet and freezing rain. Across the rest of northern Maine...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch of ice. Across eastcentral areas...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch of ice. The snow and mixed precipitation diminished during the morning and afternoon of the 21st.|Winds began to increase overnight from the 20th into the 21st...with strong winds occurring across the region during the 21st. Blizzard conditions developed across northern portions of Aroostook and Somerset counties during the 21st...with damaging wind gusts across portions of eastcentral and interior Downeast Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 11 to 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking northeast across Maine overnight on the 8th through the morning of the 9th brought heavy snow...sleet and freezing rain to northern and central areas. Across Downeast areas...mixed precipitation quickly transitioned to heavy rain. Precipitation initially developed during the afternoon of the 8th...then continued through the morning of the 9th.|A sharp gradient to storm total snow accumulations existed across Aroostook county. Storm total snow accumulations across exteme northern and northwest Aroostook county generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with locally greater totals where precipitation remained mostly snow. Snow accumulations rapidly diminished south of this area...tapering to 1 to 3 inches across southeast Aroostook county where a much greater portion of the precipitation fell in the form of sleet and freezing rain. Storm total snow accumulations across northern portions of Piscataquis and Somerset counties ranged from 6 to 8 inches. Sleet accumulations of 1 to 3 inches occurred across Aroostook county just south of the area of heaviest snow. Ice accumlations of 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch were common from southeast Aroostook...to northern Penobscot...to central Piscataquis counties with local ice accumulations up to 3/4 of an inch. Trees were snapped in this area due to the weight of the ice.|Precipitation quickly transitioned to heavy rain across Downeast areas  south of the low track. The storm brought 1 to 2 inches of rain to Downeast areas...which had received 0.50 to 1 inch of rain just 24 hours earlier from another storm which had already helped saturate conditions. In addition to the heavy rain...melting snow also contributed to runoff. The combination of the additional rain...and snow melt with clogged drains...led to urban and poor drainage flooding along with standing water in poor drainage and low lying areas across portions of Washington...Hancock and southern Penobscot counties. The high water led to some road closures near the coast. Rivers...streams and brooks were running high though generally remained within their banks. Several garage roofs across southern Piscataquis county collapsed due to the combined weight of snow and rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy rain...melting snow and clogged storm drains contributed to urban and poor drainage flooding. Total rainfall with this storm ranged from 1.00 to 2.00 inches...which was in addition to 0.50 to 1.00 inch of rain which had fallen just 24 hours earlier in a previous storm.|The urban and poor drainage flooding...along with standing water in low lying areas...limited traffic in spots and also led to some road closures. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking northeast across Maine overnight on the 8th through the morning of the 9th brought heavy snow...sleet and freezing rain to northern and central areas. Across Downeast areas...mixed precipitation quickly transitioned to heavy rain. Precipitation initially developed during the afternoon of the 8th...then continued through the morning of the 9th.|A sharp gradient to storm total snow accumulations existed across Aroostook county. Storm total snow accumulations across exteme northern and northwest Aroostook county generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with locally greater totals where precipitation remained mostly snow. Snow accumulations rapidly diminished south of this area...tapering to 1 to 3 inches across southeast Aroostook county where a much greater portion of the precipitation fell in the form of sleet and freezing rain. Storm total snow accumulations across northern portions of Piscataquis and Somerset counties ranged from 6 to 8 inches. Sleet accumulations of 1 to 3 inches occurred across Aroostook county just south of the area of heaviest snow. Ice accumlations of 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch were common from southeast Aroostook...to northern Penobscot...to central Piscataquis counties with local ice accumulations up to 3/4 of an inch. Trees were snapped in this area due to the weight of the ice.|Precipitation quickly transitioned to heavy rain across Downeast areas  south of the low track. The storm brought 1 to 2 inches of rain to Downeast areas...which had received 0.50 to 1 inch of rain just 24 hours earlier from another storm which had already helped saturate conditions. In addition to the heavy rain...melting snow also contributed to runoff. The combination of the additional rain...and snow melt with clogged drains...led to urban and poor drainage flooding along with standing water in poor drainage and low lying areas across portions of Washington...Hancock and southern Penobscot counties. The high water led to some road closures near the coast. Rivers...streams and brooks were running high though generally remained within their banks. Several garage roofs across southern Piscataquis county collapsed due to the combined weight of snow and rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with localized totals to around 17 inches.

A combination of heavy late April rains and the melting of a near record snowpack across northern portions of the county led to flooding across all of Piscataquis county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Saint John River near Dickey contributed to several hours of flooding. River levels then quickly fell with the release of the ice jam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Saint John River near Dickey contributed to several hours of flooding. At its maximum level...the river reached nearly 4 feet above flood stage. A portion of Route 161 was closed for a time during the flooding when water covered the road. River levels quickly fell with the release of the ice jam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Run-off from melting snow contributed to prolonged flooding along the Mattawamkeag River. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas...though heavy rains toward the end of the month did lead to more extensive flooding for several days late in the month. The flooding continued into early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting snow contributed to rising levels on the Mattawamkeag River which eventually led to a prolonged interval of mostly lowland flooding along the river. This flooding affected mostly the Bancroft Road between the towns of Wytopitlock and Danforth and several camps along the river. However...3.00 to 4.00 inches of rain fell across the region toward the end of the month...along with continued snow melt...which enhanced the flooding. The late month flooding led to the evacuation of 15 homes in Mattawamkeag. The flooding continued into May. The monetary value represents the total damage to public infrastructure for all of Penobscot county. In this instance the damage occurred late in the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper portion of the Mill Pond dam was partially breached...though the lower portion remained intact. A surge of water did cause some minor flooding just downstream from the dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A partial breach of the upper portion of the Mill Pond Dam led to an increase in stream levels just downstream from the dam...even though the lower portion of the dam remained intact. Some precautionary evacuations did occur. However...the upper portion of the dam stabilized several hours later with no further flooding.

There was concern for a time that the bridge might collapse...though the bridge did hold.|Between 100 and 150 homes were damaged or destroyed by the flooding in Fort Kent. Evacuations totaled between 600 and 1000 people. This was a continuation of flooding which began in late April. The monetary value represents the total damage to public infrastructure across all of Aroostook county from the late April and early May flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy late April rains and the melting of a record snowpack led to flooding of the Aroostook River at Masardis. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas along the river and Route 11 near Masardis. The flooding developed in late April and continued into early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain which fell in late April and the melting of a record snowpack led to flooding of the Aroostook River at Masardis. The river crested a little over 1 foot above flood stage. The flooding affected mostly lowland areas along the river and Route 11 near Masardis. This was a continuation of flooding which began in late April. The monetary damage represents the total damage to public infrastructure across all of Aroostook county from the late April and early May flooding.

On July 24th a closed 500 mb low was digging over NY/PA and waves of surface low pressure were riding north along the associated surface front to the west of the forecast area. A strong 50kt low level jet was also riding northward ahead of the front. This resulted in extremely high helicity values across southern and western Maine. A surface dew point boundary was in place across south central New Hampshire through northwest Maine with readings in the lower 70s to the east of this boundary and in the mid 60s to the west. Sunshine began breaking out to the east of this boundary by mid morning and convection began developing by early afternoon. Storms grew rapidly and quickly began rotating. Storm damage was exclusively related to damaging winds with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires in Cornish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southern Maine became the focus for severe thunderstorm activity as a short wave approached the region from the west. Low level shear quickly increased during the late morning and early afternoon setting the stage for a major severe weather outbreak. Large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rain were associated with these storms which began developing in the early afternoon and lasted into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Cape Elizabeth.

Large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rain were associated with these storms which began developing in the early afternoon and lasted into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees in Scarborough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 24th a closed 500 mb low was digging over NY/PA and waves of surface low pressure were riding north along the associated surface front to the west of the forecast area. A strong 50kt low level jet was also riding northward ahead of the front. This resulted in extremely high helicity values across southern and western Maine. A surface dew point boundary was in place across south central New Hampshire through northwest Maine with readings in the lower 70s to the east of this boundary and in the mid 60s to the west. Sunshine began breaking out to the east of this boundary by mid morning and convection began developing by early afternoon. Storms grew rapidly and quickly began rotating. Storm damage was exclusively related to damaging winds with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Strong.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man and a woman were killed while outside a home in Standish when lightning struck a nearby tree and travelled along the ground.  The two had gone outside in a storm to retrieve a pair of sunglasses the family dog had taken from a child when the lightning struck.

Lightning struck a tree and ignited a fire inside a small storage building about three feet away.  The storage building was used to house historic documents and items purchased or donated to the Vienna Historical Society.  While damage to the historical items could not be estimated, damage to the structure was estimated at $10,000.
An isolated severe thunderstorm moved through southwestern Maine on the evening of July 1st downing trees and wires in York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees and power lines just north of Kennebunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon of July 9th in western Maine and moved eastward through the early evening hours. Damage with these storms was exclusively due to strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees, blocking a road in Poland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing rainfall rates of 1.00 to 2.00 inches per hour caused flash flooding in the Keegan area. The combination of heavy rain and a clogged culvert caused a stream to overflow and wash across Route 1 carrying debris across the road. Traffic had to be diverted around the flooded portion of road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing rainfall rates of 1.00 to 2.00 inches per hour caused flash flooding. Several culverts and driveways were washed out by flash flooding in the Easton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remains of tropical storm Hanna moved through eastern New England dumping 5 to 7 inches of rain in western Maine in about 8 hours causing rapid rises on area streams. Road washouts were the major problem. Minor flooding occurred on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from the remnants of tropical storm Hanna resulted in minor flooding on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook, (flood stage 15.0 ft) which crested at 18.6 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossing the region...together with abundant low level moisture...combined to support the development of thunderstorms containing very heavy rain. Some of the storms produced several inches of rain per hour...with the Blue Hill area receiving up to 4.50 inches of rain in a short time. The flash flooding damaged some buildings and roads...with other roads closed due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Gardners Lake Road were damaged due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossing the region...together with abundant low level moisture...combined to support the development of thunderstorms containing very heavy rain. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing rainfall rates of several inches per hour contributed to flash flooding around Blue Hill. Rainfall totals of up to 4.50 inches were reported at Blue Hill in a short time. The heavy rains occurred at the time of high tide. With streams already full due to the high tide...runoff from the heavy rains had nowhere to drain. Storm drains were quickly overwhelmed and streams quickly overflowed. Water depths of 3 to 4 feet were reported in some low lying areas of Blue Hill submerging vehicles and flooding some buildings. A stream near the Blue Hill Memorial Hospital overflowed and flooded the basement of the hospital with around a half foot of water. A local restaurant was also flooded to a depth of nearly 2 feet. The Blue Hill town garage and water treatment plant were also damaged due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced an isolated report of large hail and several reports of hail less than .75 inches in coastal portions of southwest Maine during the morning hours of August 3rd. There were also several reports of lightning damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced up to 5 inches of rain in 2 hours causing several portions of Routes 119 and 124 to be destroyed. One bridge and several culverts were also destroyed and numerous other roads washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in the town of Minot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Hanna brought very heavy rain and flash flooding to portions of Downeast Maine. The heaviest rains and most extensive flash flooding occurred across coastal portions of Hancock and Washington counties. Storm total rainfalls of 4.00 to 6.00 inches were common across coastal portions of both Hancock and Washington counties...with local totals of 7.00 to 7.50 inches. Lesser rainfall totals were reported further inland across both counties. Many roads along the Downeast coast were closed and damaged due to the flash flooding. At Acadia National Park...flash flood related damage to roads was the worst reported in several years.|Note the monetary amounts indicated for each event in this episode was the total amount submitted by Hancock and Washington counties respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding transitioned to flooding during the morning hours. Flood waters then subsided during the early afternoon. Many roads were left damaged...debris covered and impassable once the waters receded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall of 4.50 to 6.00 inches in less than 12 hours was common across Acadia National Park from Saturday evening through mid morning Sunday...with local totals in excess of 7.00 inches. A maximum total of 7.61 inches was reported at Bar Harbor. Flash flooding was reported from Bar Harbor to Northeast Harbor where many roads were closed and damaged...with 1 to 2 feet of water covering some roads. In the Bar Harbor area...Cromwell Brook Road...Cottage Street...West Street and Route 3 were all closed due to flash flooding. Elsewhere on Mount Desert Island...Seal Cove Road was flooded and heavily damaged in spots. Additional flooding was reported at Northeast Harbor along Route 198 which was damaged and debris covered. Most of the carriage road and fire road system within Acadia National Park on Mount Desert Island was closed due to major washouts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall of 4.00 to 6.00 inches...with local totals of around 7.00 inches produced flash flooding across the region. A maximum rainfall total of 7.09 inches was reported at Lubec. Flash flooding was reported on Route 1 near Perry...near Addison...along Route 1 between Jonesboro and Whiting...near Cutler and in Machias. Roads were closed and damaged across the region...with additional damage to culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep upper level low pressure system swinging into the northeast on the 16th of August resulted in low freezing levels and weak upper flow. A strong vorticity maximum advecting into the area combined with marginal cape indices to fire off the initial convection. Terrain and potent outflow boundaries organized these initial cells into longer lived pulse type storms which quickly became numerous throughout the forecast area. Large hail was the main result of these storms with numerous reports of hail 1 inch or larger. Some flash flooding was reported due to multiple slow moving storms in Androscoggin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.25 inch hail 4 miles north of Gorham near Newhall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure lifting north through Quebec province brought strong winds to much of northern and eastern Maine through the early morning hours of the 29th. South to southeast winds increased to sustained speeds of 25 to 35 mph across the region. Estimated wind gusts approaching 60 mph occurred across much of the region...with measured wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph along portions of the Downeast coast. A peak wind gust of 65 mph was reported at West Quoddy in coastal Washington county. The strong winds snapped branches and toppled trees on power lines across the region...creating power outages. The most extensive power outages occurred across Hancock county where around 2700 customers lost power. Also in Hancock county...the ferry terminal at Bass Harbor was damaged when the strong winds caused a portion of a ramp to collapse into the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained south to southeast winds of 25 to 35 mph...with estimated gusts of around 60 mph...toppled trees and power lines producing power outages. The ferry terminal at Bass Harbor was damaged when the strong winds caused a portion of a ramp to collapse into the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches in a 12 hour period caused minor flooding on the Swift River at Roxbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Swift River at Roxbury (flood stage 7.0 ft) which crested at 8.53 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southeast winds developed in advance of a cold front crossing the region. Winds increased to sustained speeds of 30 to 40 mph...with gusts to around 60 mph...during the morning of the 26th. The strong winds then persisted through the afternoon.  A peak wind gust of 61 mph was measured at Stonington in coastal Hancock county. The strongest winds generally occurred across coastal portions of Washington and Hancock counties with the onshore winds. The strong winds snapped branches and toppled trees on power lines creating power outages.  Around 8000 customers lost power across portions of Washington...Hancock and southern Penobscot counties. Fallen trees also blocked some roads. In Bangor...a porch roof was damaged by a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with estimated wind gusts approaching 60 mph...toppled many trees and power lines producing power outages. Fallen trees blocked some roads. In Bangor...a porch roof was damaged by a falling tree.

Estimated wind gusts approaching 60 mph occurred across much of the region...with measured wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph along portions of the Downeast coast. A peak wind gust of 65 mph was reported at West Quoddy in coastal Washington county. The strong winds snapped branches and toppled trees on power lines across the region...creating power outages. The most extensive power outages occurred across Hancock county where around 2700 customers lost power. Also in Hancock county...the ferry terminal at Bass Harbor was damaged when the strong winds caused a portion of a ramp to collapse into the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained south to southeast winds of 25 to 35 mph...with estimated gusts approaching 60 mph...toppled trees and power lines producing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep upper level low pressure system swinging into the northeast on the 16th of August resulted in low freezing levels and weak upper flow. A strong vorticity maximum advecting into the area combined with marginal cape indices to fire off the initial convection. Terrain and potent outflow boundaries organized these initial cells into longer lived pulse type storms which quickly became numerous throughout the forecast area. Large hail was the main result of these storms with numerous reports of hail 1 inch or larger. Some flash flooding was reported due to multiple slow moving storms in Androscoggin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Upton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Kyle made landfall in southern Nova Scotia as a Category 1 hurricane. The storm brought tropical storm force wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph to the Downeast Maine coast. These winds and saturated ground combined to topple some trees and power lines...with around 500 customers in Hancock county losing power. Most of the power outages were near the coast where the strongest winds occurred. Based on the track of the hurricane...storm surge was not a significant factor. The most significant impact of the storm was heavy rain which contributed to flooding across portions of Hancock and Washington counties. The heavy rains occurred in two episodes. The first episode occurred around 24 hours in advance of the hurricane when tropical moisture was drawn north across the region producing 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain over much of Hancock and Washington counties. An additional 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain then fell across much of the same region with the hurricane. Combined rainfall from both events generally ranged from 3.00 to 6.00 inches across Hancock county...with local totals of around 7.00 inches. Across Washington county...total event rainfall generally ranged from 3.00 to 4.50 inches. The rain produced elevated water levels on streams and contributed to flooding of some roads and road damage. Some of the same roads which had been damagd earlier in the month were damaged again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Kyle...tracking across the eastern Gulf of Maine...brought tropical storm force wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph to coastal Washington county. The combination of gusty winds and saturated ground contributed to some trees and power lines being toppled. This led to scattered power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of very slow moving showers and thunderstorms produced 3 to 5 inches of rain which caused small rivers and streams to flood in several towns across Cumberland County. Many roads were washed out. The most severe flooding was in Freeport where almost all roads were under water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused small stream flooding in Freeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds in portions of western Maine on the afternoon and evening of June 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and power lines just west of Phillips.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Hanna brought very heavy rain and flash flooding to portions of Downeast Maine. The heaviest rains and most extensive flash flooding occurred across coastal portions of Hancock and Washington counties. Storm total rainfalls of 4.00 to 6.00 inches were common across coastal portions of both Hancock and Washington counties...with local totals of 7.00 to 7.50 inches. Lesser rainfall totals were reported further inland across both counties. Many roads along the Downeast coast were closed and damaged due to the flash flooding. At Acadia National Park...flash flood related damage to roads was the worst reported in several years.|Note the monetary amounts indicated for each event in this episode was the total amount submitted by Hancock and Washington counties respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall of 4.00 to 6.00 inches...with localized totals in excess of 7.00 inches...contributed to flash flooding across the region. Moore Road...the west entrance to Acadia National Park Schoodic Point...was washed out and impassable due to flash flooding. East Schoodic Drive...the east entrance to Acadia National Park Schoodic Point...was also washed out and impassable due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon with an upper level disturbance crossing the region. Severe thunderstorms initially developed across northern areas mid afternoon...then expanded across Downeast areas through the rest of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled in a swath 1000 feet long and 300 to 400 feet wide along Route 2A by wind gusts estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of heavy rain...along with embedded slow moving thunderstorms producing rainfall rates of 1.00 to 2.00 inches per hour...contributed to flash flooding across northwest Maine. Several small streams overflowed their banks leading to road closures and in some cases damage to roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of Wildcat Brook washed out a portion of Estcourt Road...leaving the road impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail and some flash flooding in Franklin County. Another thunderstorm produced a mudslide and road washout in southern Oxford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm resulted in a mudslide and road washout in Bethel on North Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southern Maine became the focus for severe thunderstorm activity as a short wave approached the region from the west. Low level shear quickly increased during the late morning and early afternoon setting the stage for a major severe weather outbreak. Large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rain were associated with these storms which began developing in the early afternoon and lasted into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail in Standish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Ohio River Valley and into New England, spreading heavy rain over the region. Rainfall began on the afternoon of the 28th of April and ended during the evening of the 29th. Rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 6 inches causing minor flooding on the Ellis, Swift, Androscoggin, and Kennebec Rivers. Flooding on the Androscoggin and Kennebec rivers lasted into May 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at the end of April caused flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan, (flood stage 35,000 cfs.), which crested at 69,000 cfs. The flooding lasted into the early afternoon of May 1st when the river fell below flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon with an upper level disturbance crossing the region. Severe thunderstorms initially developed across northern areas mid afternoon...then expanded across Downeast areas through the rest of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of trees were toppled or snapped in a swath approximately 5 miles long and 2 miles wide by wind gusts estimated at up to 90 mph. The trees had diameters of up to 2.5 feet. Some of the trees fell on homes and power lines. A barn also lost part of its roof.
